evals.prompt:
Evaluate this Devkind blog post against Google's Search Quality Evaluator Guidelines. The page URL is https://devkind.com.au/blog/ai-knowledge-base-ecommerce-self-updating-system and the search query that would bring a user here is 'AI knowledge base for ecommerce'. Produce a full QRG evaluation report with PQ rating, NM rating, E-E-A-T breakdown, YMYL classification, and top 3 improvements. Here is the full page content:

# AI Knowledge Base for Ecommerce: The Self-Updating System Your Team Actually Uses

Running an ecommerce business in 2026 means drowning in data. Supplier specs, competitor pricing, customer behaviour reports, seasonal trend decks, agency presentations, product research notes — scattered across Google Drive folders, email threads, and Notion pages nobody updates. Traffic to retail websites from AI sources grew 693% during the 2025 holiday season — and yet most ecommerce teams still search for answers the same way they did in 2019.

Andrej Karpathy — former OpenAI research director and one of the most respected minds in AI — recently shared a workflow that changes this entirely. Instead of using AI to write code or generate marketing copy, he uses it to maintain a living knowledge base: a self-updating wiki that gets smarter every time someone asks it a question. The model translates directly to ecommerce operations.

The problem it solves isn't exotic. Your team's best knowledge is either locked inside someone's head or buried in a folder no one can find. Both cases cost you money — in slow decisions, in duplicated research, in institutional memory that walks out the door when someone leaves.

## What an AI Knowledge Base for Ecommerce Actually Looks Like

The concept is simple enough to sketch on a whiteboard. You dump raw sources into a folder — competitor analysis reports, product data exports, market research articles, customer feedback CSVs, supplier documentation, ad creative screenshots. An LLM reads all of it and compiles a structured wiki: a collection of .md files with summaries, concept articles, and links between related ideas.

Not a static wiki you write once and neglect. A living one.

Each time you add new research, the AI updates the relevant articles, surfaces connections between ideas, and flags gaps in your knowledge. Each time your team asks a question, it reads its own index and delivers a direct answer — no complex setup required, no engineering infrastructure to maintain.

An AI-maintained knowledge base for ecommerce turns your team's scattered research into a queryable system that improves with every use and retains knowledge permanently.

More than 80% of enterprises will be running generative AI in production by 2026 (Source: Gartner) — but most are still using it for one-off tasks rather than persistent, compounding systems.

## The Four Layers Every Ecommerce AI Knowledge Base Needs

### Layer 1: Raw Data Intake
Everything goes in. Product catalogues, competitor site exports, customer support transcripts, ad performance reports, supplier PDFs, saved industry articles, LinkedIn screenshots of competitor announcements.

At Devkind, we work with ecommerce brands sitting on years of customer support data that has never been analysed. That data answers what our customers actually struggle with better than any consultant — it just needs an AI to make it queryable.

### Layer 2: AI Compilation
The AI reads raw sources and produces structured articles: competitor profiles, product spec summaries, trend reports, pricing comparisons, customer insight roundups. It cross-links related ideas automatically.

Ecommerce teams that implement AI-maintained knowledge layers reduce time spent searching for internal information by 40–60%, according to knowledge management research (livepro.com, 2025). Employees using AI tools embedded in repeating workflows report an average 40% productivity boost.

### Layer 3: Plain-English Querying
Once the wiki reaches sufficient scale — around 100 articles — the AI reads its own index and answers questions directly.

### Layer 4: Continuous Improvement Loop
The system gets smarter the more you use it. New research automatically updates existing articles. Questions the system can't answer become flagged knowledge gaps. The value compounds.

## Who Built This Concept — and Why It Works for Ecommerce

The workflow originates from Andrej Karpathy, former Director of AI at Tesla and a founding member of OpenAI. His open-source approach to building personal knowledge systems using LLMs is a direct ancestor of what enterprise AI teams are building at scale.

Devkind has adapted this concept for ecommerce operations across three client environments: a health and beauty brand (product knowledge management), a fashion retailer (trend and competitor intelligence), and a home goods business (supplier documentation and pricing analysis).

## What Most Ecommerce Businesses Get Wrong About AI Knowledge Management

The most common mistake is treating the AI knowledge base as a one-time setup project. Teams spend effort loading initial data, get good results for a few weeks, then stop feeding it. The system stagnates and eventually gets abandoned.

The second most common mistake is over-engineering the intake layer. Complex pipelines, database integrations, custom APIs — none of this is necessary at the start. A folder of files and a well-prompted LLM is sufficient to generate real business value.

The third mistake is not asking enough questions. The knowledge base improves through use. Teams that query it regularly — for competitive intelligence, for product decisions, for customer insight — compound its value faster.

## How to Get Started: A Practical Implementation Path

**Week 1:** Collect and organise. Gather your best existing research into a single folder: competitor analysis, product data, customer feedback summaries, market reports. No curation needed — everything goes in.

**Week 2:** Initial compilation. Run the source files through an LLM with a prompt that instructs it to build a structured wiki: summaries, concept articles, cross-links, and a master index.

**Week 3:** First questions. Ask the system real business questions your team would normally spend hours researching. Evaluate the quality of answers. Identify gaps.

**Week 4+:** Continuous feeding. Add new research regularly. Update the wiki. The compounding begins.

Devkind builds custom AI knowledge systems for ecommerce businesses — from initial setup through to ongoing maintenance and team training.

## The Competitive Advantage Is in the Compounding

The businesses that will dominate ecommerce in the next three years won't necessarily have the largest teams or the biggest budgets. They'll have the best institutional memory — systems that retain knowledge, surface insights, and make every decision faster and better-informed.

An AI knowledge base for ecommerce isn't a technology investment. It's an operational advantage that compounds over time.

Author: Devkind Editorial Team | Published by Devkind (devkind.com.au) — an ecommerce development agency specialising in AI-powered systems for online retail.
evals.expected_output: Structured QRG evaluation report with PQ rating (expected High or High+), NM rating for query 'AI knowledge base for ecommerce' (expected HM), E-E-A-T table breakdown, YMYL classification (expected No), and 3 specific improvement recommendations

Produce a full QRG evaluation report with PQ rating, NM rating, E-E-A-T breakdown, YMYL classification, and top 3 improvements. Here is the full page content:

# Vibe Coding vs Agentic Engineering: What Every Ecommerce Business Owner Needs to Know

70% of software teams say code quality has dropped since adopting AI tools. If your store, app, or platform is being built or maintained by a development team using AI — and virtually all of them are now — that statistic is about you.

The problem isn't AI. It's how AI is being used. And right now, there are three distinct approaches playing out across development teams globally. Understanding the difference between them is one of the most important things you can do as an ecommerce business owner in 2026.

## What Is Vibe Coding — And Why Should Ecommerce Businesses Care?

Vibe coding is the practice of prompting an AI to write code, accepting whatever it produces, and shipping it — often without a thorough review. The name says it all: it's fast, it feels productive, and it runs on vibes rather than rigour.

For a quick prototype or an internal throwaway tool, that's fine. For a production ecommerce store processing real customer payments, storing real customer data, and running real revenue — it's a different story entirely.

Vibe coding fails at production scale because it skips the three things that keep software reliable: design, review, and testing. Code that works in a demo environment collapses under real users, real security requirements, and real transaction volume.

The numbers back this up. AI-generated code has 2.74x more security vulnerabilities than human-written code. 1 in 3 vibe-coded apps ships with a serious security flaw. These aren't edge cases — they're the predictable outcome of a process that optimises for speed over quality.

For an ecommerce business, a security vulnerability isn't just a technical inconvenience. It's a data breach, a compliance failure, a reputation event, and a direct hit to customer trust.

## What Is Manual Coding — And Why Isn't It Enough Anymore?

At the other end of the spectrum is manual coding: every line written by a human engineer who understands what it does, why it's there, and how it behaves under pressure.

This approach produces genuinely high-quality, auditable, and maintainable code. Nothing is a black box. The engineer owns every decision. For critical systems — payment gateways, custom ERP integrations, complex fulfilment logic — this level of craft still matters.

Manual coding produces the most reliable software, but it doesn't scale with the speed modern ecommerce businesses demand. An experienced engineer spending a week writing boilerplate that an AI could generate in seconds is leaving competitive advantage on the table.

The reality of ecommerce development in 2026 is that 41% of all code written globally is now AI-generated. Teams that refuse to use AI at all are operating at a structural speed disadvantage. The answer isn't to avoid AI — it's to use it correctly.

## Agentic Engineering: The Approach That Delivers Both Speed and Quality

Agentic engineering is the professional standard that sits between these two extremes — and it's where serious development teams are operating right now.

The model is straightforward: AI writes the code. The engineer reviews it. Judgment stays with the human.

Agentic engineering uses AI to generate code at speed while keeping human expertise in the loop for every decision that matters. Boilerplate, repetitive logic, and scaffolding get handled instantly. Architecture decisions, security reviews, and anything touching customer data or payment flows stay in the hands of an experienced engineer.

The outcome is exactly what ecommerce businesses need: the velocity of AI-assisted development without the technical debt and security exposure that comes from shipping unreviewed code. The tools are the same as vibe coding. The process — and the results — are completely different.

There's a practical test for whether your development team is operating this way: if the engineer can't explain what the AI just wrote, they shouldn't ship it. That single standard separates agentic engineering from vibe coding in practice.

## The Real Cost of Getting This Wrong

1 in 3 vibe-coded applications ships with a serious security flaw. The cost of a data breach for an ecommerce business averages $4.45M globally (IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2024). This isn't a theoretical risk. It's a documented pattern.

For ecommerce businesses, the exposure is amplified. You're handling payment data, customer personal information, order history, and account credentials. A single insecure AI-generated endpoint is enough. The consequences — fines, reputational damage, customer churn, legal liability — compound rapidly.

## How to Evaluate Your Development Partner's Approach

Ask them: What's your process when AI writes code you didn't write yourself? A vibe coder will describe something fast, iterative, and light on process. An agentic engineer will describe a review process: code reading, testing, security checks, deployment validation.

Devkind operates as an agentic engineering firm. Every piece of AI-generated code is reviewed by an experienced engineer before it touches a client environment. The AI handles velocity. The engineer handles quality.

Author: Devkind Editorial Team | Published by Devkind (devkind.com.au) — an ecommerce development and AI systems agency.
